confirmed.description: dispatch-repair-drone (intents.ts:950) calls dispatchRepairDrone, which deducts 1 repair_pack + a distance-proportional amount of antimatter_propellant (orbital.ts:623-624), pushes a RepairDrone with an expectedArrivalTime (orbital.ts:635), AND sets sat.pendingRepairDroneId (orbital.ts:634). The drone's only completion path is tickRepairDrones (orbital.ts:639), which never runs server-side. Consequence: the repair never lands (the satellite's lodged-debris damage is never cleared), the antimatter+repair_pack are lost, and — because sat.pendingRepairDroneId stays set — every subsequent dispatch-repair-drone for that sat is rejected with 'repair-pending' (orbital.ts:599). The satellite is thus permanently locked out of repair server-side. move-satellite (intents.ts:933) has the same shape: requestSatMove sets a movingTo plan whose arrival is only resolved by tickSatMovement, so a moved sat is stuck mid-transit (and is unusable while locked:false during the move). Both are reachable by REMOTE players; the cost is deducted on success and is unrecoverable.
confirmed.evidence: intents.ts:956 `const r = dispatchRepairDrone(game.world, islandId, satId, now);` → orbital.ts:623-624 deduct repair_pack+antimatter_propellant, orbital.ts:634 `sat.pendingRepairDroneId = drone.id;`, orbital.ts:635 `world.repairDrones.push(drone)`. Completion only via tickRepairDrones (orbital.ts:639); re-dispatch guard orbital.ts:599 `if (sat.pendingRepairDroneId) return { ok: false, reason: 'repair-pending' };`. move-satellite intents.ts:940 → requestSatMove (orbital.ts:695) sets movingTo, resolved only by tickSatMovement (orbital.ts:722). No server call sites for tickRepairDrones/tickSatMovement (grep across server/ returns none). repairDrones persisted: persistence.ts:815.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Covered by the same fix: run tickRepairDrones and tickSatMovement in the server's authoritative advance so repair drones land (clearing pendingRepairDroneId) and satellite moves complete on the catch-up clock. Until the world-tick step exists server-side, these intents should arguably be gated/rejected in REMOTE rather than silently consuming resources, but the correct fix is to run the ticks.

confirmed.title: REMOTE: drone fleet ETA/progress-bar and map dot use perf-domain now against epoch-domain launchTime/expectedReturnTime — countdown shows ~1.75e9s and the dot is frozen at origin
confirmed.description: In REMOTE (the default boot mode) the server stamps drone timestamps in the EPOCH domain: ws.ts:84/86 serializes with `serializeWorld(game.world, game.islandStates, now, now)` where `now = Date.now()`, so savedAt == savedAtPerf == Date.now() (epoch). dispatchDrone (drones.ts:546-547) sets `launchTime: nowMs` / `expectedReturnTime` from that same epoch `now`. The client receives the snapshot in applyRemoteSnapshot (main.ts:2098-2100) via `deserializeWorld(snapshot, nowWall, nowWall)` with nowWall = Date.now(), so perfShift = nowPerfMs - savedAtPerf - deltaMs = nowWall - savedAt - (nowWall - savedAt) = 0 (persistence.ts:948). The drone timestamps therefore arrive UNSHIFTED, still in epoch (~1.75e12). But the drones panel renders against the PERF domain: the ticker calls `dronesUi.refresh(now)` with `now = performance.now()` (main.ts:2424, 2660) — and this refresh runs OUTSIDE the `if (!isRemote)` economy-tick guard (guard closes at main.ts:2618; refresh is at 2660), so it executes in REMOTE. renderLedgerRow computes `remainSec = max(0,(d.expectedReturnTime - nowMs)/1000)` = (1.75e12 - ~1e4)/1000 ≈ 1.75e9 s, displaying a garbage ETA like 'T-1750000000.0s'. The progress bar at drones-ui.ts:951-952 computes `elapsedMs = min(totalMs, nowMs - d.launchTime)` = min(totalMs, ~1e4 - 1.75e12) → clamped to 0 → bar stuck at 0%. The on-map drone dot uses droneCurrentPosition(d, nowMs) (drones-ui.ts:832 → drones.ts:939) with the same perf nowMs: `elapsedSec = max(0,(nowMs - d.launchTime)/1000) = 0` → travelled 0 → the dot is rendered FROZEN at the launch origin for the whole flight, then vanishes on server-side return. This is the same perf/epoch domain class as the flagged skilltree tier-reset instance, but on the drone-fleet surface, which the by-area prior audit missed. LOCAL mode is unaffected (there launchTime is minted from performance.now() at dispatch, matching the refresh clock).
confirmed.evidence: ws.ts:86 `serializeWorld(game.world, game.islandStates, now, now)` (now=Date.now() at ws.ts:84). main.ts:2100 `deserializeWorld(snapshot, nowWall, nowWall)` ⇒ persistence.ts:948 perfShift=0. drones.ts:546 `launchTime: nowMs` (epoch on server). main.ts:2424 `const now = performance.now()`; main.ts:2660 `dronesUi.refresh(now)` (outside !isRemote guard that closes at 2618). drones-ui.ts:944 `const remainSec = Math.max(0, (d.expectedReturnTime - nowMs) / 1000)`; :952 `elapsedMs = Math.max(0, Math.min(totalMs, nowMs - d.launchTime))`; drones.ts:939 `const elapsedSec = Math.max(0, (nowMs - d.launchTime) / 1000)` → 0.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Render drone time-domain UI against the wall-clock (epoch) in REMOTE, matching the domain the server timestamps live in. Thread `nowWall` (Date.now()) rather than `now` (performance.now()) into dronesUi.refresh when isRemote — or, more robustly, have applyRemoteSnapshot normalize all perf-domain fields into the client's performance.now() domain by passing the true perf clock (deserializeWorld(snapshot, Date.now(), performance.now())) so perfShift maps epoch → perf and ALL existing perf-domain UI (drones, vehicles, skilltree, maintenance) works unchanged. The latter single fix resolves the whole domain class at once; prefer it.

confirmed.title: REMOTE: settlement-vehicle ETA and progress bar compare epoch-domain launchTime/expectedArrivalTime against performance.now() — garbage countdown and stuck progress bar
confirmed.description: Same perf/epoch domain mismatch as the drone-fleet case, on the settlement-vehicle panel. In REMOTE the server's dispatch-settler intent stamps vehicle timestamps with epoch `now` (intents.ts dispatch-settler → settlement.ts dispatchVehicle sets launchTime/expectedArrivalTime from Date.now()), serialized via serializeWorld(...,now,now) at ws.ts:86 and delivered with perfShift=0 (persistence.ts:948), so vehicle.launchTime and vehicle.expectedArrivalTime stay in epoch (~1.75e12). The ticker calls `settlementUi.refresh(now)` with `now = performance.now()` (main.ts:2424, 2662), OUTSIDE the !isRemote guard (closes at main.ts:2618). renderVehicleRow then computes `remainSec = max(0,(v.expectedArrivalTime - performance.now())/1000)` ≈ 1.75e9 s (displays e.g. 'T-1750000000.0s'), and the progress fill at settlement-ui.ts:996-997 `elapsedMs = max(0, min(totalMs, performance.now() - v.launchTime))` → performance.now() - epoch is hugely negative → clamped to 0 → bar permanently 0%. A REMOTE player launching a settler ship/helicopter sees a nonsensical countdown and a never-advancing progress bar for the entire (server-authoritative, real) flight. LOCAL is unaffected. Missed by the by-area prior audit (the documented findings cover dispatch-drone payload-drop and the panel-reference staleness, not this perf/epoch render mismatch).
confirmed.evidence: settlement-ui.ts:991 `const remainSec = Math.max(0, (v.expectedArrivalTime - performance.now()) / 1000)`; :997 `const elapsedMs = Math.max(0, Math.min(totalMs, performance.now() - v.launchTime))`. main.ts:2662 `settlementUi.refresh(now)` with now=performance.now() (main.ts:2424), refresh runs outside the !isRemote block that ends at main.ts:2618. Server stamps epoch: ws.ts:86 serializeWorld(...,now,now), now=Date.now(); applyRemoteSnapshot perfShift=0 (main.ts:2100 → persistence.ts:948).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Same domain-class fix as the drone finding: either pass nowWall (Date.now()) into settlementUi.refresh in REMOTE so vehicle countdowns compare epoch-vs-epoch, or (preferred, single fix for all surfaces) change applyRemoteSnapshot to call deserializeWorld(snapshot, Date.now(), performance.now()) so every server-epoch perf-domain field is shifted into the client's performance.now() domain and all perf-domain UI works as in LOCAL.

confirmed.title: REMOTE: tier-reset cooldown gate + cooldown display in skilltree-ui compare performance.now() against epoch-domain lastResetAt — RESET button permanently locked after any reset
confirmed.description: The server's tier-reset intent stamps lastResetAt with epoch `now`: intents.ts:835 `executeTierReset(island.state, now)` where now=Date.now() (ws.ts:184/intent-runner now). The snapshot delivers it with perfShift=0 (main.ts:2100 → persistence.ts:948), so on the client lastResetAt stays in epoch (~1.75e12). skilltree-ui refreshTierReset calls `canTierReset(state, performance.now())` (skilltree-ui.ts:235-237) and tier-reset.ts:97-98 evaluates `nowMs - state.lastResetAt < TIER_RESET_COOLDOWN_MS`: with nowMs≈1e4 (perf) and lastResetAt≈1.75e12 (epoch), `1e4 - 1.75e12` is hugely negative, which is < 24h, so the gate ALWAYS reports cooldown-active — the RESET button is permanently locked in REMOTE even years after the actual 24h window elapsed. The cooldown readout at skilltree-ui.ts:240-245 computes `remaining = TIER_RESET_COOLDOWN_MS - (performance.now() - lastResetAt)` = a gigantic positive value, rendering an absurd cooldown (hundreds of thousands of hours). The server-side gate (intents.ts:833 canTierReset(state, now) with epoch now) is correct, so a player COULD still reset if the UI dispatched the intent — but the UI never enables the button. This is the perf/epoch instance the target brief referenced as 'tier-reset was one flagged instance'; it is NOT present in docs/reports/migration-findings.json and the offending comparison is unfixed at HEAD.
confirmed.evidence: skilltree-ui.ts:235 `const now = performance.now()`; :237 `const r = canTierReset(state, now)`; :240 `const elapsed = now - state.lastResetAt`; :241 `const remaining = TIER_RESET_COOLDOWN_MS - elapsed`. tier-reset.ts:97-98 `state.lastResetAt !== null && nowMs - state.lastResetAt < TIER_RESET_COOLDOWN_MS`. Server stamps epoch: intents.ts:835 `executeTierReset(island.state, now)`. Client perfShift=0: main.ts:2100 → persistence.ts:948.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Resolve via the domain-class fix in applyRemoteSnapshot (deserializeWorld(snapshot, Date.now(), performance.now()) so lastResetAt shifts into perf domain), OR pass nowWall=Date.now() into refreshTierReset/canTierReset in REMOTE. The single applyRemoteSnapshot change is preferred since it also covers persistence.ts:1093-1094 declaredAt/lastResetAt, the drone/vehicle ETA surfaces, and building maintainedAt/placedAt timers in one place.

confirmed.title: Island rename bypasses the mutation-gateway — silent data loss in REMOTE (the default mode), name reverts within ~1s
confirmed.description:
The inspector island-rename path never goes through the mutation-gateway and has no server intent, so in REMOTE mode (the default boot mode) a rename mutates only the client-local spec object, is never sent to the server, never persisted, and is overwritten by the next authoritative state push (~1s). The player types a new island name, presses Enter, sees it apply, then watches it silently revert. This is the exact class the prior by-area audit missed (cross-layer contract-parity, like place-building dropping cargoLabel): every other player mutation has a gateway method + server intent, but rename was left as a direct pure-layer mutation.

Full chain verified at HEAD:
1. inspector-ui.ts:477 `commitRename` calls the pure `renameIsland(target.spec, trimmed)` directly, mutating `spec.name` in place, then invokes `deps.onRenameIsland(target, trimmed)` (line 479).
2. main.ts:1583 `onRenameIsland: (_target, _name) => { inspector.refresh(); }` — the `_name` argument is explicitly unused; the callback only repaints DOM. Nothing is forwarded to the server.
3. mutation-gateway.ts has NO rename method — its `send(...)` calls cover 36 intents (place-building, demolish-building, relabel-cargo, expand-island, etc.) but no rename. (grep for 'rename' in mutation-gateway.ts: zero hits.)
4. server/src/game/intents.ts INTENTS table (line 180) registers 36 handlers; none is 'rename-island'. (grep for 'rename' across server/src: zero hits.)
5. `name` is an authoritative serialized field: `SerializedIslandSpec = Omit<IslandSpec, 'terrainAt'>` (persistence.ts:98), and `IslandSpec.name` is a player-mutable string (world.ts:107). serializeWorld therefore emits the server's stored name.
6. ws.ts pushes `serializeWorld(...)` every STATE_PUSH_INTERVAL_MS (default 1000ms; ws.ts:86-87, 143-146).
7. main.ts:2098 applyRemoteSnapshot does `Object.assign(worldState, d.world)` (line 2115) replacing `worldState.islands` with fresh deserialized specs, then clears+rebuilds `islandSpecsById` from them (lines 2140-2145). The fresh spec carries the server's (un-renamed) name; the local in-place edit is discarded, and the inspector's captured `target.spec` is now a stale orphan.
confirmed.evidence: inspector-ui.ts:477 `const res = renameIsland(target.spec, trimmed);` then 479 `deps.onRenameIsland(target, trimmed);`. main.ts:1583 `onRenameIsland: (_target: InspectorTarget, _name: string) => { inspector.refresh(); }`. persistence.ts:98 `export type SerializedIslandSpec = Omit<IslandSpec, 'terrainAt'>;` (name retained → pushed). ws.ts:86-87 `const snapshot = ... serializeWorld(...); socket.send(JSON.stringify({ type: 'state', snapshot }));` on a 1000ms setInterval (143-146). main.ts:2115 `Object.assign(worldState, d.world);` + 2140-2145 rebuild islandSpecsById. grep 'rename' over server/src and mutation-gateway.ts: zero matches.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a 'rename-island' intent server-side that re-validates the name via the pure `validateIslandName`/`renameIsland` in src/world.ts (same predicate the client uses, so server/client can't drift), mutates the authoritative spec.name, and persists. Add a `renameIsland(islandId, name)` method to the mutation-gateway interface (REMOTE → `send('rename-island', { islandId, name })`; LOCAL → the existing direct `renameIsland` pure call). Route inspector-ui.ts:477 and construction-ui.ts:599 through that gateway method instead of mutating the spec directly. Update SPEC.md §rename / Appendix C trust-surface to list rename as an intent. Optimistic local apply is fine but the intent must land so the next state push doesn't revert it.

confirmed.title: REMOTE mode: in-flight transport renderers interpolate wall-epoch timestamps against performance.now() — drones/vehicles freeze at origin, route ETAs garbage
confirmed.file: src/main.ts:2424,2660,2661,2662 (callsites); src/routes-renderer.ts:267,287; src/settlement.ts:769-785 (vehicleCurrentPosition) + src/settlement-ui.ts:646,991,997; src/drones.ts:938 (droneCurrentPosition) + src/drones-ui.ts:832
confirmed.description:
In REMOTE mode (the default boot mode) every in-flight transport visual is interpolated against the wrong clock domain, so route cargo chevrons, drone dots/trails, and settlement vehicle dots freeze pinned at their origin island, arrival pulses never fire, and ETA countdowns show ~1.7e9 seconds remaining.

Root cause — clock-domain mismatch between the REMOTE deserialize path and the per-frame render path:

1. The server pushes snapshots via ws.ts pushState -> catchUp(snapshot, now) -> deserializeWorld(snapshot, now, now) -> advance -> serializeWorld(world, states, now, now). Because catchUp/serialize use Date.now() for BOTH the wall and perf clock, and because every persisted snapshot has savedAt === savedAtPerf (set by serializeWorld(...,now,now) on each accepted intent and on each non-persisting read), the perfShift computed in deserializeWorld (persistence.ts:948 perfShift = nowPerfMs - savedAtPerf - deltaMs) collapses to savedAt - savedAtPerf = 0. So every transient timestamp the server emits — routes.inFlight[].arrivalTime/dispatchTime, drones[].launchTime/expectedReturnTime, vehicles[].expectedArrivalTime/launchTime — is in the WALL-EPOCH domain (~1.7e12 ms).

2. The client applies that snapshot in applyRemoteSnapshot (main.ts:2098-2100) via deserializeWorld(snapshot, Date.now(), Date.now()). Again wall==perf==now and the source was already wall-epoch, so perfShift ≈ 0 and the in-flight timestamps stay in the wall-epoch domain on the client. (Boot path main.ts:316 does the same.)

3. But the per-frame render dispatch runs OUTSIDE the `if (!isRemote)` gate (that block opens at main.ts:2444 and closes at 2618; the panel/overlay refresh at 2620-2671 runs every frame in BOTH modes). main.ts:2424 sets `const now = performance.now();` and lines 2660-2662 call dronesUi.refresh(now) / routesUi.refresh(now) / settlementUi.refresh(now) with that performance.now() value (~tens of thousands of ms since page load).

4. Those renderers interpolate position/ETA by subtracting a wall-epoch timestamp from a perf-clock now:
   - routes-renderer.ts:267 `eta = (b.arrivalTime - nowMs)/1000` ≈ (1.7e12 - 3e4)/1000 ≈ 1.7e9 s, so the arrival-pulse gate `nextEta>=0 && nextEta<=2` (line 270) never fires.
   - routes-renderer.ts:287 `t = clamp01((nowMs - b.dispatchTime)/total)` = clamp01(negative) = 0 every frame, so every in-flight chevron is drawn at the source endpoint (cx=fromX) and never advances.
   - settlement.ts vehicleCurrentPosition (used at settlement-ui.ts:646): `f = clamp(nowMs - v.launchTime)/totalMs = 0`, vehicle dot pinned at from.cx/cy; settlement-ui.ts:991 `remainSec = max(0,(v.expectedArrivalTime - performance.now())/1000)` ≈ 1.7e9 s; settlement-ui.ts:997 elapsed clamps to 0.
   - drones.ts droneCurrentPosition (used at drones-ui.ts:832): same launchTime-based interpolation against performance.now() → drone dot frozen at launch position, trail never extends.

LOCAL mode is unaffected: loadWorld() (persistence.ts:1294) calls deserializeWorld(stored) with the DEFAULT nowPerfMs = performance.now(), so perfShift = performance.now() - savedAtPerf - deltaMs rebases the saved perf-domain timestamps into the CURRENT page-load perf-domain, which is exactly what the renderers' performance.now() expects. The bug exists ONLY because REMOTE deliberately deserializes with Date.now()/Date.now() (required so lastTick/day-night math line up with the server's wall clock), which leaves transient timestamps in wall-epoch while the renderers still assume perf-epoch.

Reachability: open any REMOTE session (default), dispatch a drone / launch a settlement ship / have any route with in-flight cargo. The dots/chevrons sit motionless on the origin and ETA labels read absurd values until arrival, when the server-side advance removes them and the client re-renders the post-arrival state. The prior audit's per-intent trace only checked PERSISTED economic fields (inventory/xp); it structurally missed this transient render-time interpolation, so this is a new finding, not a regression of an existing fix.
confirmed.evidence:
persistence.ts:948  `const perfShift = nowPerfMs - snapshot.savedAtPerf - deltaMs;` with deltaMs=max(0,nowWall-savedAt); for server reads nowWall==nowPerfMs==now and savedAt==savedAtPerf ⇒ perfShift=savedAt-savedAtPerf=0.
ws.ts:85-86  `const game = catchUp(await loadSnapshot(pool, userId), now); const snapshot = ... serializeWorld(game.world, game.islandStates, now, now);` (savedAt==savedAtPerf==now).
main.ts:2100  `const d = deserializeWorld(snapshot, nowWall, nowWall);` (nowWall=Date.now()) ⇒ in-flight times stay wall-epoch.
main.ts:2424  `const now = performance.now();`  main.ts:2660-2662 `dronesUi.refresh(now); routesUi.refresh(now); settlementUi.refresh(now);` run every frame (gate `if(!isRemote)` closes at 2618).
routes-renderer.ts:267 `const eta = (b.arrivalTime - nowMs) / 1000;`  :287 `const t = Math.max(0, Math.min(1, (nowMs - b.dispatchTime) / total));`
settlement.ts (vehicleCurrentPosition) `const elapsedMs = Math.max(0, Math.min(totalMs, nowMs - v.launchTime));`  settlement-ui.ts:991 `const remainSec = Math.max(0, (v.expectedArrivalTime - performance.now()) / 1000);`
drones-ui.ts:832 `const pos = droneCurrentPosition(d, nowMs);` with nowMs=performance.now().
confirmed.suggestedFix: Feed the render path a clock in the SAME domain the deserialized in-flight timestamps live in. In REMOTE mode those timestamps are wall-epoch, so the per-frame transport-render dispatch must pass Date.now() (a wall clock) instead of performance.now(). Concretely: at the main.ts ticker (around 2424/2431 nowWall already exists), pass `isRemote ? nowWall : now` to dronesUi.refresh / routesUi.refresh / settlementUi.refresh (lines 2660-2662) so the interpolation `nowMs - launchTime` / `arrivalTime - nowMs` uses matching epochs. Equivalently, make the REMOTE deserialize rebase transient timestamps into the client's performance.now() domain (pass nowPerfMs=performance.now() while keeping nowWall=Date.now() for the economy lastTick), but that splits perf/wall and would also need the day-night/lastTick remap audited — the cleaner fix is the render-clock selection. Add a REMOTE render-mode test asserting that a route in-flight batch's interpolation parameter t advances over wall-clock time (not pinned at 0) when timestamps are wall-epoch, and that vehicleCurrentPosition/droneCurrentPosition move off the origin.

confirmed.title: Server never calls tickRoutes — a draining (deleted) route is never pruned and persists forever, while LOCAL splices it out; REMOTE re-adds the 'deleted' route to the client every snapshot
confirmed.description: The server's only economy step in catchUp() is advanceWorldEconomy() (runtime.ts:43), which does NOT call tickRoutes — grep confirms tickRoutes is referenced nowhere in server/ except a (false) comment at intents.ts:641. tickRoutes is the ONLY place that prunes a draining route (src/routes.ts:822-824: splice when draining && inFlight.length===0). So server-side, delete-route (intents.ts:649) sets route.draining=true and the route then lives in game.world.routes permanently — it is never removed. LOCAL deleteRoute (mutation-gateway.ts:461-463) hard-splices a route with zero in-flight cargo immediately. Because the server is the only place routes are ever dispatched/delivered (and it never ticks them either), a deleted route on REMOTE always has inFlight.length===0 yet is never pruned. serializeWorld spreads the full route (persistence.ts:791 `...r`, including `draining` and `cargo`) into every 1Hz WS state push (ws.ts:86-87), and applyRemoteSnapshot rebuilds world.routes from it, so the route the player just deleted reappears on the client on the next snapshot, flagged DRAINING — a ghost route the player cannot remove.
confirmed.evidence: runtime.ts:43 `advanceWorldEconomy(world, islandStates, now, now);` is the sole advance; `grep -rn tickRoutes server/ --include=*.ts | grep -v test` → only intents.ts:641 (a comment). routes.ts:822-824 prune is reachable only via tickRoutes. intents.ts:649 `route.draining = true;` with no later removal path server-side.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either prune in delete-route directly when no in-flight cargo (mirror LOCAL: if route.inFlight.length===0 splice from game.world.routes, else set draining), or have the server run a route prune/tick step in catchUp so draining routes with 0 in-flight are removed. Update the intents.ts:641 comment which currently asserts a server-side prune that does not exist.

confirmed.title: §3.6 island merge never runs in REMOTE (default) mode — whole spec mechanic silently disabled server-side
confirmed.description: In the server-authoritative (REMOTE = default) boot mode, §3.6 Island Joining never happens. The client's merge step (findNextMerge/performMerge in src/main.ts:2489-2495) lives INSIDE the `if (!isRemote)` economy block (src/main.ts:2444), so it is skipped entirely when the server is authoritative. The server side never compensates: `catchUp`/`advanceWorldEconomy` (server/src/game/runtime.ts:43) calls the shared pure economy advance, which contains zero merge calls (`grep merge src/economy-advance.ts` -> nothing), and there are no merge references anywhere under server/src (`grep -rn Merge server/src` -> nothing). Crucially this is REACHABLE today, not a future-tick concern: the server exposes an `expand-island` intent (server/src/game/intents.ts:587) that authoritatively calls `expandIsland`, growing an island's ellipse via the Land Reclamation Hub — the exact mechanic §3.6 says triggers a merge when the grown island touches a neighbor. A REMOTE player can grow two populated islands into geometric overlap and the islands will simply never join: persisted geometry stays as two overlapping-but-separate islands forever, contradicting SPEC §3.6 and the documented invariant in src/island-merge.ts that 'the economy ticker calls findNextMerge once per tick after advanceIsland.' This is a server-migration regression of behavior that worked in the pre-migration client-authoritative model.
confirmed.evidence: src/main.ts:2444 `if (!isRemote) {` guards the whole block that at :2489 does `const merge = findNextMerge(worldState, islandStates);` then :2495 `performMerge(...)`. server/src/game/runtime.ts:43 `advanceWorldEconomy(world, islandStates, now, now);` is the only advance the server runs; `grep -n merge src/economy-advance.ts` returns nothing and `grep -rn 'Merge|merge' server/src | grep -v test` returns nothing. expand-island intent: server/src/game/intents.ts:587 calls `expandIsland(island.spec, island.state, axis)`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Make merge resolution part of the shared server-authoritative advance so REMOTE matches LOCAL. Either (a) lift the findNextMerge/performMerge loop out of main.ts's `!isRemote` block into a pure step inside advanceWorldEconomy (run once per advance, at-most-once-per-tick per §3.6) so both client-LOCAL and server use the same path; or (b) run the merge loop in server/src/game/runtime.ts catchUp after advanceWorldEconomy (and persist it via loadAndCatchUp). Update SPEC.md §3.6 / Appendix C to state where merge resolution executes in the server-authoritative model.

confirmed.title: performMerge leaves satellite/repair-drone/lattice/comm-packet island references dangling — orphans orbital + lattice state
confirmed.description: performMerge remaps routes (from/to), drones (fromIslandId), and vehicles (target/from) when an island is absorbed, but it does NOT remap four other stored island-id references, all of which become dangling the moment a merge runs (in LOCAL mode today, and in REMOTE the instant the missing server-side merge from finding #1 is wired): (1) Satellite.spaceportIslandId (src/orbital.ts:76) — after absorption the absorbed id is gone from world.islands and world.islandStates, so every owner-state lookup returns undefined: tickRepairDrones (orbital.ts:657,673) falls back to reliabilityMul=1 / reserveMul=1, silently wiping the owner island's Resilience-skill repair reliability and satFuelReserve bonuses; the sat's ground-station comm range and skill-derived buffer benefits are lost. The satellite is permanently orphaned from the surviving (absorber) island that should now own it. (2) world.latticeNodeIslands (src/world.ts:727) — if the absorbed island was a T5 Lattice node, its id stays in the array but crossIslandNeighbors (src/lattice.ts:103-104) `world.islands.find` returns undefined and latticeStorageCaps `islandStates.get` returns undefined, so it contributes nothing; meanwhile the absorber (which subsumed it) is NOT added to latticeNodeIslands, so the merged super-island drops out of the Lattice's shared-building / summed-storage pool. (3) CommPacket.currentNodeId (src/orbital.ts:67) — island ids are comm-graph node ids (orbital.ts:438-442); a packet sitting at the absorbed island's node is dropped as 'packet lost' (orbital.ts:491) instead of being delivered through the absorber. (4) These are never tested: src/island-merge.test.ts always builds worlds with empty satellites/repairDrones/latticeNodeIslands/commPackets (test:110-112), and performMerge's own docstring (island-merge.ts:76-100) enumerates only routes/drones/vehicles — the orbital+lattice references were missed. The crossedCells recompute-from-geometry fix (4b7f8a8) already showed merge interacts badly with persisted transport geometry; this is the same class of gap for orbital/lattice references.
confirmed.evidence: performMerge handles routes (island-merge.ts:235-257), drones (:260-265), vehicles (:268-278) but has no handling for world.satellites, world.repairDrones, world.latticeNodeIslands, or world.commPackets. Satellite.spaceportIslandId is consumed at orbital.ts:657 `world.islandStates?.get(sat.spaceportIslandId)` and orbital.ts:673; lattice stale-id at lattice.ts:103-104; comm-packet drop at orbital.ts:491,505 `if (!graph.has(pkt.currentNodeId)) ...lost`. island-merge.test.ts:110-112 constructs every test world with `satellites: [], repairDrones: [], latticeNodeIslands: [], commPackets: []`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In performMerge, after the existing drone/vehicle remaps: (1) loop world.satellites and rewrite `spaceportIslandId === absorbed.id -> absorber.id` (Satellite.spaceportIslandId is readonly, so rebuild the object like the drone path); (2) loop world.commPackets and rewrite `currentNodeId === absorbed.id -> absorber.id`; (3) for latticeNodeIslands, replace the absorbed id with absorber.id and de-dupe (if absorber already present, just drop the absorbed entry). RepairDrone references sats by targetSatId not island id, so it follows the sat remap transitively — no direct change needed, but confirm with a test. Add merge tests that seed a satellite, a comm packet, and a lattice membership on the absorbed island and assert they retarget to the absorber. Extend the performMerge docstring step list to cover these.

confirmed.title: Inspector panel freezes on a stale orphaned IslandState/PlacedBuilding/IslandSpec after every REMOTE snapshot swap
confirmed.description:
In REMOTE mode the inspector's display silently freezes ~1 second after a building is selected, showing stale numbers (rates, inventory, level, maintenance timer, floor count, force-run/cargo-label state) that never update again until the building is re-clicked, even though the server keeps pushing fresh state every second.

Root cause is object-identity churn the panel never re-resolves. mountInspectorUi keeps a module-scoped `let target: InspectorTarget | null` (inspector-ui.ts:297). `open(t)` sets `target = t` once (1962-1969); the captured `target.spec`, `target.state`, `target.building` are direct references to specific IslandSpec/IslandState/PlacedBuilding objects taken from the world at click time (main.ts:844,882 pass `island`, `targetState`, `hitBuilding`). `paint()` (called every frame by `inspector.refresh()` at main.ts:2680) destructures `const { spec, state, building } = target` (inspector-ui.ts:1388) and reads/recomputes everything off those exact objects — e.g. `computeRates(state, ratesCtx, undefined, Date.now())` at line 1461, `state.inventory[...]`, `state.level`, `building.constructionRemainingMs/forceRun/cargoLabel/disabledFloors`.

But applyRemoteSnapshot replaces those very objects. `Object.assign(worldState, d.world)` (main.ts:2115) swaps `worldState.islands` for d.world's FRESH spec array, with FRESH PlacedBuilding objects inside each spec.buildings. `islandStates.set(id, st)` (main.ts:2125-2127) overwrites each Map value with a FRESH deserialized IslandState (it does not mutate the existing object in place). The economy advance loop that would otherwise mutate these objects is `if (!isRemote)`-gated (main.ts:2444), so in REMOTE mode applyRemoteSnapshot is the ONLY state-update path — once it runs, `target.{spec,state,building}` are detached, garbage-collected-once-dereffed orphans that receive no further updates. refreshRetainedRates (main.ts:2370 `for (const s of islandStates.values())`) correctly iterates the LIVE swapped states for the HUD, so the HUD updates while the inspector diverges — confirming the panel is reading a parallel frozen copy.

The in-place-reconcile comment at main.ts:2094-2117 reasons only about the `worldState` container and the `islandStates` Map *object identities* (which ARE preserved). It does not hold for the inspector, which captured the INNER state/building/spec objects whose identities are NOT preserved.

Contrast the merge path (main.ts:2510-2524), which DOES handle exactly this hazard for re-minted ids by re-resolving via islandSpecsById.get(...).buildings.some(...) and closing on miss — applyRemoteSnapshot has no equivalent re-resolution or staleness check.
confirmed.evidence: inspector-ui.ts:1388 `const { spec, state, building } = target;` then 1461 `const rates = computeRates(state, ratesCtx, undefined, Date.now());` and 1462 `rates.byBuilding.find((r) => r.building.id === building.id)` — all off captured target objects. main.ts:2115 `Object.assign(worldState, d.world);` (fresh islands/buildings arrays). main.ts:2125-2127 `for (const [id, st] of d.islandStates) { islandStates.set(id, st); }` (fresh state per id). main.ts:2444 `if (!isRemote) { if (forceEconomyTick || shouldTick(...)) { ... advanceEconomy(...) } }` — no local mutation of target objects in REMOTE. main.ts:2680 `inspector.refresh();` every frame. Compare merge re-resolution at main.ts:2515-2521.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Re-derive the inspector target from live containers each paint instead of caching the inner objects. Two viable shapes: (a) store only `{islandId, buildingId}` in InspectorTarget and have paint() resolve `state = islandStates.get(islandId)` / `spec = world.islands.find(id)` / `building = spec.buildings.find(id)` at the top, closing the panel if any resolve fails (mirrors the merge guard at main.ts:2515-2521); or (b) have applyRemoteSnapshot, after reconciling, call an inspector API that re-resolves and re-points target by its current `getSelectedIslandId()`/`getSelectedBuildingId()` (and closes on miss), exactly as the merge path does. Option (a) also fixes the LOCAL-fallback relabel write at inspector-ui.ts:806-807 landing on a building object that a future swap could orphan. Add a REMOTE test: select a building, apply a snapshot that changes that building's inventory/forceRun, assert the inspector readout reflects the new values.

confirmed.title: relocate-building: server coerces omitted rotation to 0, dropping the pure-fn's preserve-current-rotation default (LOCAL/REMOTE divergence)
confirmed.description: The gateway contract types relocate rotation as optional (src/mutation-gateway.ts:118-124, `rotation?: Rotation`). The pure ground-truth fn `relocateBuilding(spec, state, id, newX, newY, rotation?)` defaults an omitted rotation to the building's CURRENT rotation: `const rot = (rotation ?? b.rotation ?? 0)` (src/placement.ts:1062) — i.e. omitting rotation means 'keep the building's existing orientation'. The LOCAL gateway path honors this by forwarding `rotation` unchanged, including `undefined` (src/mutation-gateway.ts:263). The REMOTE path serializes the payload via JSON.stringify in the WS client (src/server-client.ts:162), which DROPS an `undefined` rotation key, so the server handler receives `payload.rotation === undefined`. The server then coerces it BEFORE the pure call: `const rot = (rotation ?? 0) as Rotation;` (server/src/game/intents.ts:528) and passes `rot` (=0) to relocateBuilding (line 529). This bypasses the pure fn's `?? b.rotation` branch, forcing rotation to 0 instead of preserving the building's current rotation. Net: for a relocate intent that omits rotation, LOCAL preserves orientation while REMOTE resets it to 0 — a behavior divergence in exactly the dropped/wrong-default class the audit targets. This is latent today because the sole production caller (src/placement-ui.ts:785) always passes a concrete Rotation (initialized to 0 at line 247, set to `building.rotation ?? 0` on relocate-arm at line 611), so no live UI path currently triggers it; but the contract admits undefined, the pure default is real and test-exercised (src/placement.test.ts:2269 etc. call relocateBuilding without rotation), and any future caller relying on omit-to-preserve would silently lose orientation only in REMOTE.
confirmed.evidence: src/mutation-gateway.ts:561 `return send('relocate-building', { islandId, buildingId, x, y, rotation });` (rotation may be undefined) → src/server-client.ts:162 `socket!.send(JSON.stringify({ type, payload, seq: mySeq }))` (drops undefined key) → server/src/game/intents.ts:528 `const rot = (rotation ?? 0) as Rotation;` vs pure src/placement.ts:1062 `const rot = (rotation ?? b.rotation ?? 0) as Rotation;` and LOCAL src/mutation-gateway.ts:263 passing rotation through unchanged.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Forward the optional rotation to the pure fn the same way LOCAL does, letting the pure default fire. In server/src/game/intents.ts replace lines 528-529 with: `const rot = (rotation === undefined ? undefined : (rotation as Rotation)); const r = relocateBuilding(island.spec, island.state, buildingId, x, y, rot);` so an omitted rotation reaches relocateBuilding as undefined and preserves `b.rotation`, matching LOCAL and the pure contract. (Validation at line 523 already rejects out-of-range non-undefined values, so this is safe.)

confirmed.title: REMOTE rebuild gate (discoverySignature) omits island ellipse radii — expand-island geometry repaint relies entirely on the vision-source signature
confirmed.description:
The 'expand-island' intent's field-correspondence trace is clean across all 4 layers: the client REMOTE path sends send('expand-island', { islandId, axis }) (mutation-gateway.ts:578-580); the LOCAL path calls expandIsland(island.spec, island.state, axis) (mutation-gateway.ts:305-310); the server handler destructures and validates exactly { islandId, axis } and calls expandIsland(island.spec, island.state, axis as Axis) (intents.ts:587-601); and the pure fn expandIsland(spec, state, axis) (land-reclamation.ts:165) takes no nowMs/timestamp, so nothing is dropped, arity/order match, and the server's extra canExpandIsland reason-surfacing pre-check yields the same end-state as LOCAL (the pure fn self-guards with the same canExpandIsland). No payload-drop bug exists for expand-island.

The latent defect is one layer up, on the REMOTE display side. expandIsland mutates ONLY spec.majorRadius/minorRadius (land-reclamation.ts:179-180). In REMOTE the only world-layer repaint path is applyRemoteSnapshot (main.ts:2098), which gates rebuildWorldLayers() on `changed = visionSig !== lastVisionSig || discoverySig !== lastDiscoverySig` (main.ts:2167). discoverySignature() (main.ts:2076-2090) folds in discovered count, revealedCells.size, per-island populated/discovered/modifiers and per-building geometry — but NOT spec.majorRadius/minorRadius/extraEllipses, even though renderIsland recomputes computeIslandTiles from those radii on each rebuild. So an expand's geometry repaint is NOT covered by the structural (discovery) half of the gate; it is covered ONLY by the vision-source half, which happens to change because a populated island's vision ellipse major = spec.majorRadius + VISION_PADDING_TILES (lighthouse.ts:65, world.ts:190). For the realistic case (expand target has a Land Reclamation Hub and is therefore a player-controlled, populated island) the rebuild fires via visionSig and the user sees the new size. The gap is unmasked only for a non-populated island carrying a Hub (not in computeVisionSources' `populated` filter, main.ts:2164) — a state not reachable by normal play, hence minor.
confirmed.evidence: discoverySignature() builds `${s.id}:${s.populated?1:0}:${s.discovered?1:0}:${s.modifiers.join(',')}#` plus per-building `${b.id}@${b.x},${b.y}/...` (main.ts:2081-2086) — no majorRadius/minorRadius/extraEllipses term. expandIsland mutates `spec.majorRadius = spec.majorRadius + 1` (land-reclamation.ts:179). Rebuild gate: `const changed = visionSig !== lastVisionSig || discoverySig !== lastDiscoverySig;` then `if (changed) rebuildWorldLayers();` (main.ts:2167-2173). Masking dependency: vision ellipse `major: c.major + VISION_PADDING_TILES` with c.major = spec.majorRadius (lighthouse.ts:65 + world.ts:190), and computeVisionSources is fed only `worldState.islands.filter((s) => s.populated)` (main.ts:2164).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add the island ellipse geometry to discoverySignature so the structural half of the REMOTE rebuild gate covers a §3.4 expansion directly rather than depending on the vision-source halo growing. Append `:${s.majorRadius},${s.minorRadius}` (and a digest of extraEllipses) to each island's segment in discoverySignature() (main.ts:2081). This is defense-in-depth: it makes the geometry repaint robust regardless of whether the expanded island is in the populated/vision-source set, matching LOCAL where expandIsland's spec mutation is immediately followed by rebuildWorldLayers() in onExpandIsland (main.ts:1560-1574).

confirmed.title: Server intent 'cancel-queued-upgrade' is a dead, exact duplicate of 'cancel-construction' — no client path reaches it
confirmed.description:
Strict 4-layer trace of "cancel-queued-upgrade":

(1) Client gateway REMOTE path: There is NO client gateway method that sends 'cancel-queued-upgrade'. `grep -rn 'cancel-queued-upgrade' src/` returns zero matches. The only cancel-related send in the client is `cancelConstruction(islandId, buildingId)` which sends `send('cancel-construction', { islandId, buildingId })` (src/mutation-gateway.ts:567).

(2) Client gateway LOCAL path (ground truth): `cancelConstruction(islandId, buildingId)` resolves the island and calls the pure fn `cancelConstruction(island.spec, island.state, buildingId)` (src/mutation-gateway.ts:272-276). The pure fn (src/placement.ts:1181, signature `(spec, state, buildingId)`) peels the newest queued upgrade job first, then falls back to fresh-placement cancel — so a single client method covers BOTH cancel-construction and cancel-queued-upgrade semantics. There is no distinct client 'cancelQueuedUpgrade' method.

(3) Server handler 'cancel-queued-upgrade' (intents.ts:608-619): destructures `{ islandId, buildingId }`, validates both are strings, resolves island, calls `cancelConstruction(island.spec, island.state, buildingId)`.

(4) Server pure-fn call: identical pure fn `cancelConstruction(spec, state, buildingId)`.

The 'cancel-queued-upgrade' handler is byte-for-byte identical to the 'cancel-construction' handler (intents.ts:285-296) — same destructure, same two string validations, same `resolveIsland`, same pure-fn call with same arg order/arity. The client never emits the 'cancel-queued-upgrade' type, so the intent is orphaned/dead. PARITY VERDICT for the path the client actually uses (cancelConstruction -> 'cancel-construction'): CLEAN. No dropped field, no wrong default, no arity/order mismatch, no missing nowMs (the pure fn takes no timestamp), and LOCAL vs REMOTE call the identical pure fn with identical args. The defect is purely the duplicate dead intent, which has no user-facing failure path.
confirmed.evidence: src/mutation-gateway.ts:567 `return send('cancel-construction', { islandId, buildingId });` is the only cancel send; `grep -rn 'cancel-queued-upgrade' src/` => no matches. server/src/game/intents.ts:608-619 ('cancel-queued-upgrade') is identical to intents.ts:285-296 ('cancel-construction'): both call `cancelConstruction(island.spec, island.state, buildingId)`. Only server/src/game/intents.test.ts:643-671 references the dead intent. Pure fn signature src/placement.ts:1181-1185 `cancelConstruction(spec, state, buildingId)`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Remove the duplicate 'cancel-queued-upgrade' handler (and its colocated test block) since the client routes both fresh-placement and queued-upgrade cancels through 'cancel-construction', whose pure fn already handles the LIFO queued-upgrade peel. Alternatively, if a distinct intent name is intended for forward use, document it as reserved — but as-is it is unreachable code that can mislead a maintainer into believing the client sends it.

confirmed.title: Route weather-loss path recompute-from-geometry diverges from the persisted dispatch-time path for batches in flight across an island merge
confirmed.description: 4b7f8a8 stopped persisting per-batch crossedCells and recomputes them in deliverArrivals via routeCrossedCells(route, islandIndex), which reads the route's CURRENT route.from/route.to island centres. The old design stored each batch's crossedCells computed at DISPATCH time from the then-current endpoints. The commit claims behavioral identity 'for static island centres', but island merge (island-merge.ts:236-257) redirects a third-party route's endpoint (e.g. X->B becomes X->A when B is absorbed into A) while KEEPING the route's in-flight batches (the `...r` spread preserves inFlight). A batch dispatched along X->B before the merge, delivered after, now has its §2.6 weather-loss roll recomputed against A's centre (X->A geometry) instead of B's old centre — different rasterized cells, different per-cell biome/CO2 weather sampling, different loss amount. Pre-refactor that batch kept its original-endpoint path. So the loss roll for batches in flight at the moment of a merge silently changed. The dispatch-time capacity throttle was never affected (it always recomputed via routeCapacityMultiplierForWeather), so the only behavioral surface is delivery loss on cross-merge in-flight batches.
confirmed.evidence: routes.ts:150 `return rasterizeRouteCells(fromSpec.cx, fromSpec.cy, toSpec.cx, toSpec.cy, CELL_SIZE_TILES);` keyed on route.from/route.to live; island-merge.ts:247-249 mutates `from`/`to` to absorber.id but newRoutes keep inFlight; pre-refactor dispatch attached `crossedCells: d.crossedCells` (4b7f8a8 diff) frozen at dispatch endpoints.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either (a) accept and document the divergence in SPEC §2.6 (the loss difference is small and merges are rare), or (b) at merge time, flush/deliver or re-stamp in-flight batches on redirected routes so the recomputed geometry matches what's expected; or (c) make routeCrossedCells take a per-batch endpoint hint when a route's endpoints have changed since dispatch. The cleanest is to document it as an accepted approximation since the impact is bounded to in-flight batches at the instant of a merge.

confirmed.title: REMOTE rebuild gate folds many non-visual building fields, over-rebuilding GPU textures on force-run / relabel / pause toggles (perf re-regression of the 61000c2 fix)
confirmed.description: 61000c2 added the rebuild gate specifically to stop re-baking ocean/island/fog GPU textures on every 1 Hz idle push. e7f3964 widened the gate's fingerprint to detect building changes that repaint the island sprite. But the actual building sprite painter, renderBuildings (the only thing rebuildWorldLayers re-bakes for buildings), draws each building from ONLY defId (footprint/fill/stroke/glyph), x, y, rotation — it ignores paused, forceRun, cargoLabel, anchorIslandId, constructionRemainingMs. The new discoverySignature folds all of paused, forceRun (`f`), cargoLabel, anchorIslandId, and a construction boolean into the per-island fingerprint. Construction progress, invalid markers, etc. are drawn by building-alerts-overlay which refreshes EVERY frame independently (main.ts:2671), not via rebuildWorldLayers. So a server-side force-run toggle, a cargo relabel, an ocean-pause flip, or construction-complete now triggers a FULL ocean+island+fog GPU re-bake (rebuildWorldLayers destroys/recreates all gradient textures) even though none of those change any pixel produced by renderIsland/renderBuildings — re-introducing exactly the GPU churn 61000c2 set out to eliminate, just gated on a different (still-too-broad) set of events.
confirmed.evidence: discoverySignature (main.ts:2083-2085) appends `${b.paused ?? ''}:${b.forceRun ? 'f' : ''}:${b.cargoLabel ?? ''}:${b.anchorIslandId ?? ''}` and a construction `'c'/'o'` flag; renderBuildings (buildings.ts:368-431) reads only def (from defId), b.rotation, b.x, b.y. building-alerts-overlay.refresh(now) runs every frame at main.ts:2671 and owns the construction/invalid visuals.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Trim the structural fingerprint to the fields renderBuildings/renderIsland actually consume: defId, x, y, rotation, plus island populated/discovered/modifiers and tileOverrides (terrain visuals). Drop paused, forceRun, cargoLabel, anchorIslandId and the construction boolean from the gate — those have no effect on the re-baked layers (they are either non-visual or drawn by per-frame overlays).

confirmed.title: REMOTE delete-route never removes an empty route (sets draining only); LOCAL splices it immediately
confirmed.description: The `delete-route` intent unconditionally sets `route.draining = true` (intents.ts:649) regardless of whether the route has in-flight cargo. The LOCAL gateway (mutation-gateway.ts:461-466) splices the route out of `world.routes` immediately when `inFlight.length === 0`, and only sets `draining = true` when cargo is in flight. Normally `tickRoutes` would prune a drained-and-empty route, converging the two paths — but per the finding above the server never calls `tickRoutes`, so server-side a deleted empty route lingers in `world.routes` forever with `draining = true`. Even once the tick gap is fixed, the immediate-vs-deferred removal is an observable parity difference for an empty route (LOCAL: gone on next push; REMOTE: gone only after a tick). Low impact (draining routes are hidden in the routes UI) but it is a genuine LOCAL/REMOTE divergence the migration introduced.
confirmed.evidence: intents.ts:642-651 delete-route: `route.draining = true; return { ok: true };` (no empty-route splice). mutation-gateway.ts:456-468 LOCAL deleteRoute: `if (route.inFlight.length === 0) { ... world.routes.splice(idx, 1); } else { route.draining = true; }`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Mirror the LOCAL gateway in the intent: when `route.inFlight.length === 0`, splice the route out of `game.world.routes`; otherwise set `route.draining = true`. (Best paired with restoring the server-side tickRoutes call so draining routes are actually pruned.)

confirmed.title: Expired/revoked sessions are never reaped and per-user session count is unbounded — sessions table grows without limit
confirmed.description: `createSession` inserts a new row on every login/signup (auth/routes.ts:32) with a 30-day TTL (cookie.ts:4). Nothing ever deletes expired or revoked sessions: `findValidSession` only filters them out at read time and `revokeSession` sets `revoked_at` but keeps the row. There is no reaper job, no `DELETE FROM sessions WHERE expires_at < now()`, and no per-user session cap. A user who logs in repeatedly (each login mints a fresh session rather than reusing one) accumulates rows indefinitely; over months/years the table grows unbounded and the per-user `sessions_user_id_idx` scans in findValidSession touch ever more dead rows. Not directly exploitable for takeover, but a slow storage/perf leak with no upper bound, introduced by the migration's session model.
confirmed.evidence: sessions.ts:8-11 INSERT only; revokeSession (sessions.ts:30-34) UPDATEs revoked_at, no DELETE. routes.ts:30-34 issueSession always creates a new row. No reaper anywhere (no DELETE FROM sessions in server/src or migrations). Migration 0001_init.sql:12-22 has no TTL/cleanup mechanism.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a periodic reaper (cron or a `setInterval` on boot) running `DELETE FROM sessions WHERE expires_at < now() OR revoked_at IS NOT NULL AND revoked_at < now() - interval '7 days'`, and/or cap sessions per user (delete the oldest beyond N on createSession). Reusing/refreshing an existing valid session on login instead of always inserting would also bound growth.

confirmed.title: Account enumeration via signup status code (409 vs 201) and via cross-endpoint timing on duplicate signup running a full scrypt
confirmed.description: The login path is timing-equalized against the dummy hash (good), but signup still leaks account existence: POST /api/auth/signup with an already-registered email returns 409 `email already registered` vs 201 for a new email — a direct enumeration oracle, gated only by the 10/min per-IP auth rate limit. Additionally createUser computes the full async scrypt (routes.ts:38 `await hashPassword(...)`) BEFORE the unique-violation surfaces from the INSERT, so a duplicate-email signup costs one full KDF; combined with the distinct status code this is an enumeration surface an attacker can use to harvest valid emails (then target the rate-limited login). Standard hardening (generic response, or signup that doesn't reveal existence) is absent.
confirmed.evidence: routes.ts:42 `if (err instanceof EmailTakenError) return reply.code(409).send({ error: 'email already registered' });` — distinct from the 201 success path (routes.ts:40). users.ts:18-21 maps PG 23505 to EmailTakenError only after the INSERT, which runs after hashPassword. Auth rate limit is 10/min/IP (app.ts:30-33) — enumeration is throttled but not prevented.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Return a generic 200/202 'if this email is new, an account was created; otherwise nothing changed' style response, or at minimum keep the status uniform and avoid signaling existence. Consider tightening the auth rate limit and/or adding per-account lockout. Enumeration on signup is lower-stakes than login but should be acknowledged in the trust-surface doc.

confirmed.title: SPEC Appendix C still documents `trustProxy: true` after the fix changed it to `trustProxy: 1` — code and spec out of sync
confirmed.description: The critical XFF-spoofing finding was fixed in f1368db by changing server/src/app.ts from `trustProxy: true` to `trustProxy: 1` (app.ts:20, with a long comment explaining why `true` lets a client spoof req.ip via leftmost X-Forwarded-For). But SPEC.md Appendix C still asserts the opposite: 'Fastify runs with `trustProxy: true` (`server/src/app.ts`) so the auth rate-limit buckets on the real client IP from `X-Forwarded-For`'. This is now flatly false against the code — `true` is precisely the value the fix removed because it does NOT bucket on the real client IP (it trusts the attacker-controlled leftmost XFF entry). Per AGENTS.md 'Source of truth' ('Every change that alters behavior must update SPEC.md in the same change — code and spec move together'), the f1368db fix should have updated this Appendix C note. The deploy README (server/deploy/README.md:15-26) WAS correctly updated to `trustProxy: 1` + `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $remote_addr`, so only the SPEC is stale.
confirmed.evidence: SPEC.md:2314 `Fastify runs with \`trustProxy: true\` (\`server/src/app.ts\`) so the auth rate-limit buckets on the real client IP from \`X-Forwarded-For\``. vs server/src/app.ts:20 `const app = Fastify({ logger: false, trustProxy: 1 });`. deploy/README.md:17 correctly says `The app runs with \`trustProxy: 1\``.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Update SPEC.md:2314-2316 to state `trustProxy: 1` (count of trusted hops = single nginx) and the rationale that `true` would let a client spoof req.ip via the leftmost X-Forwarded-For; reference the nginx `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $remote_addr` requirement so the spec matches app.ts and the deploy README.

confirmed.title: SPEC Appendix C claims every server snapshot 'triggers a full world-layer rebuild' after the fix gated the rebuild on actual vision/discovery change
confirmed.description: SPEC Appendix C boot-flow step 4 states: 'Each subsequent server-pushed snapshot replaces the client's world state and triggers a full world-layer rebuild.' The 'triggers a full world-layer rebuild' clause is now false: the performance fix in 61000c2/e7f3964 changed applyRemoteSnapshot to (a) mutate worldState in place (NOT 'replace' it — that was the orphaned-overlay regression fix) and (b) gate rebuildWorldLayers() behind an actual vision-source/discovery signature change (main.ts:2163-2173 `if (changed) rebuildWorldLayers();`), exactly because unconditionally rebuilding churned the GPU at 1 Hz at idle. So a snapshot whose vision/discovery state is unchanged does NOT rebuild the world layers, and the world state is updated in place rather than replaced. The SPEC sentence describes the pre-fix behavior the migration audit explicitly removed. Minor, but it is a doc claim that contradicts the current code per the repo's code/spec-move-together rule.
confirmed.evidence: SPEC.md:2286-2287 `Each subsequent server-pushed snapshot replaces the client's world state and triggers a full world-layer rebuild.` vs main.ts:2115 `Object.assign(worldState, d.world);` (in-place, not replace) and main.ts:2167-2172 `const changed = visionSig !== lastVisionSig || discoverySig !== lastDiscoverySig; ... if (changed) { rebuildWorldLayers(); }` (gated, not unconditional).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Reword SPEC.md:2286-2287 to: each subsequent snapshot is applied in place onto the existing world state (preserving subsystem references), and a world-layer rebuild fires only when the vision-source set or discovery/revealed-cell state changes (matching main.ts applyRemoteSnapshot).

confirmed.title: Draining route stays fully mutable on REMOTE — set-route-cargo / set-route-mode / set-cargo-weight / set-cargo-floor-pct / reorder-route-cargo accept edits to a route the player already deleted; the routes-UI renders the cargo editor for draining routes
confirmed.description: None of the five route-mutation intents guard against route.draining: set-route-cargo (742-753), set-route-mode (657-667), set-cargo-weight (673-689), set-cargo-floor-pct (694-711) and reorder-route-cargo (717-735) only check that the route exists via resolveRoute, then mutate it. Combined with the finding above (a draining route is never pruned server-side and is re-pushed to the client), the routes-UI compounds it: renderLedgerRow calls renderCargoEditor(route, ...) unconditionally at routes-ui.ts:780 — it is NOT gated on route.draining (only the delete button is swapped for a DRAINING badge at routes-ui.ts:700-731). So a player sees the full add/remove/weight/floor/reorder/mode controls on a route flagged DRAINING and every edit succeeds server-side. LOCAL would have already removed that route (no panel, no possible edit), so the two layers disagree on whether the route exists and on its cargo/mode. The cargo edits are also pointless work since dispatchPhase skips draining routes (routes.ts:853), but the server still accepts and persists them.
confirmed.evidence: intents.ts:747-751 — set-route-cargo resolves route and replaces cargo with no `if (route.draining)` check; same shape in the other four handlers. routes-ui.ts:780 `renderCargoEditor(route, row, () => refresh(performance.now()));` runs for every row including draining ones (the draining branch at 700-731 only changes the delete control).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Reject mutations on a draining route in all five intent handlers (e.g. `if (route.draining) return { ok:false, error:'route is draining' }` after resolveRoute), and gate routes-ui.ts:780 so renderCargoEditor is skipped (or read-only) when route.draining. Best paired with the prune fix above so draining routes don't linger to be edited at all.

confirmed.title: create-route accepts unvalidated filterResource, minting a route with a bogus cargo resourceId that violates the isValidResourceId invariant
confirmed.description: The 'create-route' intent validates filterResource is a string (intents.ts:436-437) but never checks isValidResourceId before casting `filterResource as ResourceId` (intents.ts:458) and passing it to createRouteFromBuilding, which builds `cargo: [{ resourceId: filter }]` (src/routes.ts:1090). Every other resource-bearing intent in this file gates the id: set-defaults cargoLabel (intents.ts:207), relabel-cargo newLabel (intents.ts:565), and validateCargoList for set-route-cargo (intents.ts:115) all reject unknown resource ids via isValidResourceId. So create-route is the one resource entrypoint that lets a crafted intent inject a CargoEntry whose resourceId is not a real resource. The server framing is anti-cheat ('client cannot forge state'), yet this forges a route holding an invalid cargo invariant directly into authoritative game.world.routes, where it is persisted with no load-time re-validation (server/src/game/persistence.ts has no cargo resourceId re-gate; routes.ts deserialize does not re-validate). Downstream the bogus entry is inert (planCargo's tryPush returns early because inv(srcState, r) <= 0 for a resource that can never exist — src/routes.ts:612-613), so it is not an exploit for resource gain; the defect is a durable authoritative-state integrity violation and an inconsistency: set-route-cargo would reject the very same entry that create-route accepts. Note this gap is shared by the LOCAL path too (mutation-gateway.ts:434-454 and createRouteFromBuilding likewise skip validation), so it is NOT a REMOTE-vs-LOCAL divergence; it is a missing authoritative input-validation gate. The UI restricts the cargo dropdown to real resources (routes-ui.ts:576/588), so it is reachable only by a crafted intent.
confirmed.evidence: server/src/game/intents.ts:436-437 `if (filterResource !== undefined && typeof filterResource !== 'string') return {ok:false,...}` then :458 `const filter = filterResource === undefined ? null : (filterResource as ResourceId);` — no isValidResourceId, unlike intents.ts:115 (`if (resourceId !== CARGO_WILDCARD && !isValidResourceId(resourceId)) return {ok:false,...}`) and intents.ts:207/565. The minted entry: src/routes.ts:1090 `cargo: filter !== null ? [{ resourceId: filter }] : []`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In the 'create-route' handler, after the typeof check, reject an unknown filter: `if (filterResource !== undefined && !isValidResourceId(filterResource)) return { ok: false, error: 'unknown filterResource' };` (mirroring intents.ts:115/207/565). Optionally apply the same guard at the createRouteFromBuilding seam so the LOCAL path is consistent.

confirmed.title: create-route requires BOTH endpoints populated server-side, but LOCAL createRoute does not — a latent REMOTE-only eligibility asymmetry
confirmed.description: The authoritative create-route enforces §2.4 eligibility by rejecting when either endpoint is not populated (intents.ts:446-447). The LOCAL mutation-gateway createRoute (src/mutation-gateway.ts:434-454) performs NO populated check on fromSpec or toSpec — it only verifies the islands and source building exist, then calls createRouteFromBuilding and pushes. The eligibility filtering lives only in the routes-UI dropdown (routes-ui.ts:436-439 populatedIslands), so the two authoritative mutation seams disagree on what is a legal route. In normal play the UI gates both modes identically, so this is not currently a user-reachable desync; it is a latent contract divergence: any path that calls gateway.createRoute with a depopulated endpoint (e.g. a route to an island that was depopulated by island-merge/settlement after the cached dropdown was built — the routes-ui comment at lines 1044-1048 explicitly anticipates 'settlement (§12) starts mutating populated mid-game') succeeds in LOCAL but is rejected in REMOTE, producing mode-dependent behavior for the same action. The server is arguably the more spec-correct side (§2.4), so the right harmonization is to add the populated gate to the LOCAL/pure seam, not relax the server.
confirmed.evidence: server intents.ts:446 `if (!fromSpec.populated) return {ok:false,...}` / :447 `if (!toSpec.populated) return {ok:false,...}`. LOCAL src/mutation-gateway.ts:434-453 resolves fromSpec/toSpec and the building, computes dist, and pushes the route with no `.populated` check anywhere. routes-ui.ts:1044-1048 comment confirms populated is expected to become dynamic.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add the §2.4 endpoint-populated check to the LOCAL createRoute (mutation-gateway.ts) — or, better, into createRouteFromBuilding / a shared eligibility helper used by both seams — so LOCAL and REMOTE accept/reject the identical route set. Update SPEC.md §2.4 reference if the populated requirement is being formalized as the single eligibility rule.

confirmed.title: §9.8 Trade Offers flagged L=live in SPEC but wholly non-functional in REMOTE (the default boot mode); SPEC never updated to record the migration gap
confirmed.description:
SPEC.md's Implementation Status legend (line 13) defines L = live, P = partial, N = not implemented. §9.8 Trade Offers is flagged L (line 48) as a fully live mechanic (Signal Exchange building + online-only expiring barter offers, with a multi-paragraph normative §9.8 at lines 1279-1344). But after the server-authoritative migration the mechanic is entirely dead in REMOTE, which is the DEFAULT boot mode (remote-boot.ts; SPEC §migration line 2290 'REMOTE default'):

1. Offers never SPAWN in REMOTE. The only code that populates tradeRuntime.offers is tickTradeOffers, which sits inside the `if (!isRemote)` block (src/main.ts:2444 opens the gate, the tickTradeOffers call is at 2475-2482). In REMOTE the economy tick is skipped entirely (SPEC line 2293 'The local simulation tick is skipped in REMOTE mode') and the server runtime never re-runs trade-offer generation (no trade logic in server/src/game/), so tradeRuntime.offers stays [] forever.
2. The gateway accept path is a hard stub: src/mutation-gateway.ts:665-666 acceptTrade() returns Promise.resolve({ok:false, error:'not supported yet'}).
3. There is no server intent to accept or reject a trade. server/src/game/intents.ts:968-971 explicitly documents 'UNWIRED: reject-trade ... Wired only when server-deterministic trade offers land.' and there is no accept-trade intent at all.

So a live-flagged whole spec mechanic is silently absent for every default (REMOTE) player. Per AGENTS.md ('code and spec move together ... any divergence is a bug'), the migration changed the effective behavior of §9.8 in the default mode without a SPEC update. The §migration section (SPEC lines 2182-2314) describes the REMOTE architecture in detail but lists no REMOTE-disabled mechanics, so a reader has no way to know §9.8 is dead in REMOTE. This is the spec-drift residue of the prior audit's minor product-gap finding ('Trade reject in REMOTE mutates island state directly; trade accept silently no-ops') whose own suggestedFix said 'document the gap in SPEC.md so code and spec stay in sync' — the client-side handlers were fixed (commit 61000c2 added the isRemote toast guards at src/main.ts:1964-1967 and 1986-1989, which hold at HEAD), but the SPEC documentation half of that fix was never applied (git show --stat 61000c2 shows it touched src/main.ts, src/mutation-gateway.ts, and the test — not SPEC.md).

Wired only when server-deterministic trade offers land.' (and no 'accept-trade' key exists in the intent table). Either (a) downgrade §9.8 from L to P with a Notes carve-out stating trade offers are LOCAL-only post-migration and non-functional in REMOTE (the default mode) until server-deterministic trade intents land, and add a one-line entry to the §migration REMOTE-disabled-mechanics list; or (b) implement server-side trade-offer generation + accept-trade/reject-trade intents and route the gateway through them, then keep §9.8 as L. Option (a) is the low-risk reconciliation; the code comments at src/main.ts:1959-1963 and server/src/game/intents.ts:968-971 already describe the intended deferral, so the SPEC just needs to match.
